title: Nutrition: Fats and Carbohydrates
content:
FATS

Essential for: Fuel, insulation, cushioning internal organs, lubrication, absorption of fat-soluble vitamins.

Saturated fats: Dairy products, animal fats, coconut oil, hydrogenated fats. Tend to raise blood cholesterol.

Polyunsaturated fats: Vegetable oils (safflower, corn, soybean). Tend to lower cholesterol if taken in double proportion to saturated fats.

Monounsaturated: Peanut, olive oils—almost neutral effect.

Hidden fats: Hamburgers and doughnuts (¼ fat), chocolate and egg yolk (⅓), bacon and peanut butter (½), pecans and some nuts/seeds (¾).

All fats sensitive to high temperatures, light, air. Store carefully; do not reach smoking point in cooking. US fat consumption has risen from 20% to over 40% of calories in 20 years.

CARBOHYDRATES

Classes: Starches and sugars.

Sugars:
- Monosaccharides: Fruit sugars, honey (sweeter)
- Disaccharides: Table sugar
- Polysaccharides: Starches (must be broken down before body use)

Chewing importance: Starch breakdown begins with enzyme in saliva. Complex carbohydrates should be thoroughly chewed.

Caloric values: Fruits and vegetables often lower than cereals; concentrated sweets higher.

US sugar consumption: Increased 25% since 1900s; 103 pounds per capita annually (mostly in commercially prepared foods).
